walkthrough.title: Assemble the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Tzolk'in set up. First, take the four interlocking board pieces and assemble them in the numbered order printed on the backs: piece 1 first, then 2, then 3, then 4. If this is your very first game, you also need to attach the six gears to the board using the plastic fasteners, and apply the stickers. The large central gear is the Tzolk'in calendar wheel with 26 teeth. The five smaller gears represent the five locations: Palenque, Yaxchilan, Tikal, Uxmal, and Chichen Itza. Make sure each gear meshes with the central wheel. Once attached, the gears stay on permanently.
walkthrough.image_caption: Game board with six interlocking gears attached

walkthrough.title: Set the Calendar
walkthrough.text: Now rotate the large Tzolk'in gear until the arrowhead on the board points at one of the two teeth marked with a blue-green sticker. These blue-green teeth represent the end-of-age Food Days. The game always begins with the arrow pointing here. Important: even though the arrow points to a Food Day tooth, the first round is not a Food Day. That tooth represents the final Food Day, which occurs only after the gear completes a full revolution.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tzolk'in gear set to the starting position

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Jungle (Harvest Tiles)
walkthrough.text: Look at the Palenque gear area. You will see four groups of four field spaces next to the gear, corresponding to Palenque actions 2, 3, 4, and 5. Place one Corn Harvest Tile on each of the four fields in every group. That uses 16 Corn Harvest Tiles total. Now, for the groups corresponding to actions 3, 4, and 5, place one Wood Harvest Tile on top of each Corn Harvest Tile. That uses 12 Wood Harvest Tiles. The group for action 2 gets only corn tiles, no wood on top. For a 3-player game, use only 3 fields per group instead of 4. For a 2-player game, use only 2 fields per group.
walkthrough.image_caption: Harvest Tiles placed on jungle fields near Palenque

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Corn Bank
walkthrough.text: Place all 65 corn tokens in the center of the Tzolk'in gear. These tokens come in values of 1 and 5. This corn bank is the shared supply that everyone draws from and pays into throughout the game. Place it where all players can easily reach it.
walkthrough.image_caption: Corn tokens in the center of the Tzolk'in gear

walkthrough.title: Create the Resource Bank
walkthrough.text: Set out the 65 wooden resource blocks near the board in a shared bank. These come in three types: wood, stone, and gold. Also place the 13 crystal skulls in the bank. Important: there are exactly 13 crystal skulls in the entire game. Once the last skull is taken, no one can gain any more. Wood, stone, gold, and corn are unlimited. If you run out of wooden blocks, use a suitable substitute.
walkthrough.image_caption: Resource bank with wood, stone, gold, and crystal skulls

walkthrough.title: Separate and Shuffle Buildings and Monuments
walkthrough.text: Find the 32 building tiles and 13 monument tiles. Separate them into three face-down stacks by their backs: Age 1 buildings, Age 2 buildings, and monuments. Shuffle each stack separately. Deal 6 monuments face up into the monument spaces on the board. For a 3-player game, deal only 5 monuments. For a 2-player game, deal only 4. Return unused monuments to the box without looking at them. Then deal 6 Age 1 buildings face up into the building row on the board. Keep both the Age 1 and Age 2 building stacks face down near the board.
walkthrough.image_caption: Monuments and Age 1 buildings dealt face up on the board

walkthrough.title: Choose Colors and Distribute Player Components
walkthrough.text: Each player chooses a color and takes the following in that color: 1 player board (lighter side up to start), 3 workers (the other 3 stay in the bank for now), 7 markers, and 1 scoring counter. Place your scoring counter on space 0 of the scoring track around the board edge.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player board and components in one color

walkthrough.title: Place Player Markers on Temples and Technologies
walkthrough.text: Each player places their 7 markers as follows: 1 marker on the white starting step of each of the 3 temples (Chaac on the left, Quetzalcoatl in the middle, Kukulcan on the right). That is 3 markers on temples. Then place 1 marker on the leftmost starting space of each of the 4 technology tracks (Agriculture, Resource Extraction, Architecture, Theology). That is 4 markers on technology tracks. All 7 markers are now placed.
walkthrough.image_caption: Markers placed on temples and technology tracks

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Wealth Tiles
walkthrough.text: Mix up all 21 Starting Wealth Tiles face down. Deal 4 tiles secretly to each player. Each player looks at their 4 tiles and chooses 2 to keep, discarding the other 2 face down. Once everyone has chosen, all players reveal their 2 chosen tiles simultaneously. Each player receives the items or bonuses shown on their tiles. Some tiles give corn, resources, technology advances, temple steps, extra workers, or crystal skulls. Keep your Starting Wealth Tiles face up in front of you. Some tiles have ongoing effects that can be used during the game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Starting Wealth Tiles showing various bonuses

walkthrough.title: Handle Dummy Workers (2-3 Player Games Only)
walkthrough.text: If playing with fewer than 4 players, you need to place dummy workers to block some action spaces. After dealing Starting Wealth Tiles to each player but before they choose which 2 to keep, take workers in the unused player colors. Draw the remaining Starting Wealth Tiles one at a time. Each tile shows a specific action space on one of the 5 gears. Place a dummy worker on that space. If it is the first dummy on that gear, also place a second dummy on the opposite action space of the same gear (exception: not on Chichen Itza). Continue until you have placed 6 dummy workers for a 3-player game or 12 for a 2-player game. Dummies stay on gears for the entire game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Dummy workers blocking action spaces in a 3-player setup

walkthrough.title: Determine the Starting Player
walkthrough.text: Give the Starting Player Marker to the player who most recently sacrificed something. If there is a tie, give it to whoever volunteers for the next sacrifice. The Starting Player Marker does not rotate automatically between rounds. It only changes hands when someone places a worker on the Starting Player Space between the Palenque and Chichen Itza gears. You are now ready to play.
walkthrough.image_caption: Starting Player Marker

walkthrough.title: Round Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Each round has up to three phases. First, all players take their turns starting with the starting player and going clockwise. Second, if this round is a Food Day, players must feed their workers and receive temple rewards. Third, the Tzolk'in calendar gear advances one day, pushing all workers on all gears forward by one action space. The game lasts for one full revolution of the Tzolk'in gear, which takes 26 rounds. There are 4 Food Days during the game, dividing it into two ages.
walkthrough.image_caption: Overview of the round phases

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Place or Pick Up
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you must do exactly one of two things: either place one or more workers onto the gears, or pick up one or more workers from the gears. You cannot do both on the same turn, and you cannot skip your turn. Your very first turn of the game will always be a placement turn because you start with no workers on the gears. If all your workers are on gears, you must pick some up. If you have workers both on and off the gears, you choose which action to take.
walkthrough.image_caption: Place workers or pick them up - never both

walkthrough.title: Placing Workers
walkthrough.text: When you choose to place workers, you must place at least one. You may place multiple workers on the same turn, even all of your available workers. Each worker goes on the lowest-numbered unoccupied action space on whatever gear you choose. You cannot pick which numbered space to place on. You always go to the lowest available. You pay two costs when placing: first, the number printed on the action space where each worker lands (action space 0 costs 0, space 1 costs 1, and so on). Second, a placement fee based on how many workers you place this turn, shown on your player board: 1 worker costs 0 extra, 2 workers costs 1 extra, 3 workers costs 3 total extra, and so on. You cannot place more workers than you can afford. You cannot choose to place zero workers.
walkthrough.image_caption: Worker placement costs: action space number plus placement fee

walkthrough.title: Begging for Corn
walkthrough.text: At the start of your turn, before you place or pick up, if you have 2 or fewer corn, you may beg for corn. Discard all your corn and take 3 corn from the bank. This angers the gods, so you must move down 1 step on one temple of your choice. You cannot beg if you are already at the bottom step of every temple. There is one rare special case: if you have no workers on the gears and not enough corn to place even one worker, you must beg. And if you are at the bottom of all temples and cannot beg, the gods take pity and let you place exactly 1 worker on the cheapest available space, paying all your corn.
walkthrough.image_caption: Begging for corn: discard all corn, take 3, step down a temple

walkthrough.title: Picking Up Workers and Performing Actions
walkthrough.text: If you have at least one worker on the gears, you may pick up workers instead of placing. You can pick up one, some, or all of your placed workers. For each worker you pick up, you choose one of three options: perform the action at the space where the worker was standing, perform a lower-numbered action on the same gear by paying 1 corn per step back, or perform no action at all. You resolve each worker's action sequentially in whatever order you choose. The order can matter because resources gained from one worker can be used to pay costs for the next. The highest-numbered action spaces on each gear (6-7 on most gears, 10 on Chichen Itza) are free-choice actions: you may perform any one action on that gear without paying extra corn.
walkthrough.image_caption: Pick up workers, choose actions, resolve in order

walkthrough.title: The Gear Mechanism
walkthrough.text: After all players have taken their turns, the Tzolk'in gear rotates one day counter-clockwise. Because the smaller gears interlock with it, all workers on all five location gears advance one action space forward. A worker that was on action space 1 is now on action space 2. This means workers become more valuable the longer they stay on the gears. However, a worker on the highest-numbered action space (7 on most gears, 10 on Chichen Itza) will be pushed off the gear. That worker returns to its owner with no action. Plan ahead so you pick up your workers before they fall off. Dummy workers in 2-3 player games are never pushed off.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tzolk'in gear rotation advances all workers one space

walkthrough.title: Palenque: Harvesting Food and Wood
walkthrough.text: Palenque is all about harvesting. Action 1 is fishing, which gives you 3 corn and never runs out. Actions 2 through 5 correspond to groups of jungle fields with Harvest Tiles. When you use one of these actions, pick a field in that group and remove the top tile. If it is a Wood Harvest Tile, take wood (2 wood for action 3, 3 wood for action 4, 4 wood for action 5). If it is a Corn Harvest Tile, take corn (4 for action 2, 5 for action 3, 7 for action 4, 9 for action 5). On actions 3, 4, and 5, you can also burn the forest: discard the Wood Tile on top, take the Corn Tile beneath it, and gain the corn. But burning angers the gods, so you must step down one temple. Actions 6-7 are free-choice actions. Keep your Harvest Tiles because some monuments score based on them.
walkthrough.image_caption: Palenque gear: fishing, harvesting corn, harvesting wood

walkthrough.title: Yaxchilan: Gathering Resources
walkthrough.text: Yaxchilan offers resources from the mountains. Action 1 gives 1 wood. Action 2 gives 1 stone and 1 corn. Action 3 gives 1 gold and 2 corn. Action 4 gives 1 crystal skull. Action 5 gives 1 gold, 1 stone, and 2 corn. Actions 6-7 are free-choice actions, letting you perform any single Yaxchilan action without paying extra. Remember there are only 13 crystal skulls in the game. Once the 13th is taken, action 4 has no effect.
walkthrough.image_caption: Yaxchilan gear: wood, stone, gold, crystal skulls

walkthrough.title: Tikal: Building and Technology
walkthrough.text: Tikal is the center of development. Action 1 lets you advance one level on one technology track, paying the appropriate number of resource blocks. Action 2 lets you construct one building by paying its resource cost. Action 3 lets you advance 1 or 2 technology levels (either on one track or split across two), paying for each. Action 4 lets you construct one or two buildings, or one monument. If building two, architecture technologies apply to only one of them. Action 5 lets you climb 1 step on two different temples, costing 1 resource block. Actions 6-7 are free-choice actions. When you construct a building, deal a replacement from the current age stack at the end of your turn.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tikal gear: technology, construction, temples

walkthrough.title: Uxmal: Commerce and Offerings
walkthrough.text: Uxmal is the commercial hub. Action 1 lets you climb 1 step on one temple for 3 corn. Action 2 lets you exchange corn and resources as many times as you want using the exchange rate table on the board. Action 3 lets you take one of your workers from the bank and put it in front of you, expanding your workforce up to 6 total. Action 4 lets you construct a building, but you pay entirely in corn: 2 corn per resource in the building's cost. You cannot use resources, and you cannot build monuments this way. Action 5 lets you perform one action from the Palenque, Yaxchilan, Tikal, or Uxmal gear for 1 corn (Chichen Itza is excluded). Actions 6-7 are free-choice actions.
walkthrough.image_caption: Uxmal gear: temple offerings, market, recruiting, corn construction

walkthrough.title: Chichen Itza: Crystal Skulls
walkthrough.text: Chichen Itza is the sacred site for crystal skull offerings. This gear has action spaces numbered 1 through 10 (larger than the others). Each action space has an oval for one crystal skull. When you perform an action, you must place a crystal skull from your supply into that oval. If someone else already placed a skull there, you cannot use that action. Each action is usable only once per game. When you place a skull, you receive immediate rewards: a specific number of victory points, a step up on a specific temple, and sometimes a resource block of your choice. Each action is tied to one of the three gods: Chaac, Quetzalcoatl, or Kukulcan. Action 10 is a free-choice action.
walkthrough.image_caption: Chichen Itza gear: place skulls for VP, temple steps, and resources

walkthrough.title: The Starting Player Space
walkthrough.text: Between the Palenque and Chichen Itza gears, there is a special Starting Player Space. It costs 0 corn to place a worker here (you still pay the placement fee for number of workers placed). When placed here, you receive several benefits. Immediately after your turn, take all corn that has accumulated on the teeth of the Tzolk'in gear. At the end of the round during calendar advancement: your worker returns to you automatically, the Starting Player Marker changes hands (you take it if you don't have it, or pass it left if you do), and you may choose to advance the calendar 2 days instead of 1, but only if your player board is lighter-side-up and the extra day would not push any worker off a gear. If you use this speed-up privilege, flip your board to the darker side.
walkthrough.image_caption: Starting Player Space between Palenque and Chichen Itza

walkthrough.title: Technology Tracks
walkthrough.text: There are four technology tracks, each with three levels plus a repeatable bonus at the top. To advance from start to level 1 costs 1 resource block. From level 1 to 2 costs 2 blocks. From level 2 to 3 costs 3 blocks. Blocks can be any mix of wood, stone, and gold. Once at level 3, you cannot advance further, but each time you would advance, you pay 1 block and gain a one-time bonus. Agriculture technology gives extra corn when harvesting. Resource Extraction gives extra resources from Yaxchilan and Palenque. Architecture gives discounts and bonuses when constructing buildings. Theology gives crystal skull bonuses and lets you look ahead on Chichen Itza. All tech benefits are cumulative with lower levels.
walkthrough.image_caption: Four technology tracks with three levels each

walkthrough.title: Temples
walkthrough.text: The three temples track your standing with the gods. Chaac (brown, left) has the fewest steps but is easier to climb. Quetzalcoatl (yellow, middle) is medium height. Kukulcan (green, right) is the tallest. The top step of each temple can be occupied by only one player. All other steps can hold multiple players. When you reach the top step, immediately flip your player board to the lighter side, which allows you to use the calendar speed-up privilege again. If you anger the gods (by burning forests or begging for corn), you step down 1 step on a temple of your choice. You cannot choose a temple where you are already at the bottom.
walkthrough.image_caption: Three temples of varying heights with player markers

walkthrough.title: Food Days: Feeding Workers
walkthrough.text: There are 4 Food Days per game, marked by stickers on the Tzolk'in gear. Two are brown-orange (middle of each age) and two are blue-green (end of each age). On a Food Day, after all players take their turns, each player must pay 2 corn per worker in play. A worker is 'in play' if it is in front of you or on a gear. Workers still in the bank do not need food. If you cannot feed all your workers, you lose 3 victory points for each unfed worker. You must feed as many as you can. Certain buildings called farms reduce feeding costs. Victory points can go negative.
walkthrough.image_caption: Food Day: pay 2 corn per worker in play

walkthrough.title: Food Days: Temple Rewards
walkthrough.text: After feeding, the gods give rewards based on your temple positions. On brown-orange Food Days (middle of each age), you receive resources and crystal skulls. For each temple, you get the resource depicted on your current step and all resources from lower steps. On blue-green Food Days (end of each age), you receive victory points. You score only the VP shown on your current step, not the steps below. Additionally, the highest-ranked player on each temple receives a bonus: the lower-left number at end of Age 1, the upper-right number at end of Age 2. If players tie for highest, all tied players get half the bonus (rounded down).
walkthrough.image_caption: Temple rewards: resources at mid-age, VP at end-of-age

walkthrough.title: Age Transition and Building Changes
walkthrough.text: At the second Food Day (end of Age 1), after feeding and rewards, remove all remaining Age 1 buildings from the board and deal 6 Age 2 buildings face up into the building row. Monuments are not affected. For the rest of the game, only Age 2 buildings are available. Age 2 buildings are generally more powerful but more expensive.
walkthrough.image_caption: Age 1 buildings removed, Age 2 buildings dealt

walkthrough.title: Buildings and Monuments
walkthrough.text: Buildings come in several types. Single-use buildings give immediate bonuses when constructed: corn, resources, temple steps, victory points, technology advances, or workers. Farms give ongoing benefits that reduce feeding costs on Food Days. Buildings are replenished at the end of your turn from the current age stack. Monuments are available from the start and are never replenished. Monuments score victory points at the end of the game based on various conditions: number of buildings constructed, harvest tiles collected, technology levels reached, temple positions, workers in play, or crystal skulls placed. You can only construct monuments with Tikal action 4, and architecture technology bonuses do not apply to monuments.
walkthrough.image_caption: Buildings give immediate bonuses; monuments score at game end

walkthrough.title: Game End and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the fourth Food Day, when the Tzolk'in gear has made one complete revolution. After the final end-of-age scoring for temples, perform final scoring. First, convert all remaining resources to corn using the market exchange rate. Second, score 1 victory point for every 4 corn you have (including corn from converted resources). Third, score 3 victory points for each crystal skull you still possess. Fourth, score victory points from any monuments you constructed. The player with the most total victory points wins. In case of a tie, the player with the most workers still on the gears wins. If still tied, all tied players share the victory.
walkthrough.image_caption: Final scoring: resources to corn, corn to VP, skulls, monuments

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Mayan Calendar
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first two rounds with four players: Red, Blue, Green, and Yellow. Green is the starting player. Each player begins with 3 workers, some corn from their Starting Wealth Tiles, and markers on the temples and technology tracks. Follow along to see how placing workers, picking them up, and the gear rotation all work together. One of our players will make some classic beginner mistakes along the way.
walkthrough.image_caption: Four-player setup ready for round 1

walkthrough.title: Round 1: Green Places Three Workers
walkthrough.text: Green is the starting player and has the first turn of the game. She wants to get workers onto multiple gears early. She places one worker on Tikal action space 0, one on Yaxchilan action space 0, and notices that adding a third worker on Palenque action space 0 costs only 2 more corn. All three spaces cost 0 corn each, and placing 3 workers costs 3 corn total from the placement fee. Green pays 3 corn. Good move. She has workers on three different gears that will advance each round.
walkthrough.image_caption: Green places three workers on action space 0 of three gears

walkthrough.title: Round 1: Blue Places Two Workers
walkthrough.text: Blue places two workers on Palenque. The first goes to action space 1 (the lowest unoccupied, since Green took space 0). The second goes to action space 2. That costs 1 + 2 = 3 corn for the action spaces, plus 1 corn for the two-worker placement fee, for 4 corn total. Blue would like a third worker on Palenque but does not have enough corn. Smart to stay within budget.
walkthrough.image_caption: Blue places two workers on Palenque spaces 1 and 2

walkthrough.title: Round 1: Red's Mistake - Overspending on Placement
walkthrough.text: Red is excited and places three workers: two on Yaxchilan action spaces 1 and 2, and one on Tikal action space 1. The cost is 1 + 2 + 1 = 4 corn for the action spaces, plus 3 corn for the three-worker placement fee, totaling 7 corn. Red started with only 8 corn from Starting Wealth Tiles and now has just 1 corn left. That is dangerously low. If Red cannot pick up workers next turn to earn corn, she may have to beg for corn and lose a temple step.
walkthrough.image_caption: Red overspends on worker placement, down to 1 corn

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Manage Your Corn Reserves
walkthrough.text: Here is the lesson from Red's mistake. Corn is everything in Tzolk'in. It is your food to feed workers, your currency to place workers, and your emergency fund. Spending down to 1 corn in Round 1 is risky because you might need corn to pay for a lower action when picking up workers, or to feed workers on a Food Day. Always keep a small corn buffer. As a rule of thumb, try to keep at least 3 corn in reserve during the early game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Keep a corn reserve - never spend down to zero

walkthrough.title: Round 1: Yellow Places Workers and Takes Starting Player Space
walkthrough.text: Yellow places one worker on Tikal action space 2 and another on the Starting Player Space (which costs 0). Placing 2 workers costs him 2 + 0 = 2 for action spaces, plus 1 for the placement fee, totaling 3 corn. At the end of the round, Yellow will get the Starting Player Marker and any corn accumulated on the Tzolk'in gear.
walkthrough.image_caption: Yellow takes the Starting Player Space

walkthrough.title: Round 1: End of Round - Gear Advances
walkthrough.text: All players have taken their turns. Now the calendar advances. Yellow's worker is on the Starting Player Space, so Yellow gets the Starting Player Marker and their worker returns automatically. Yellow may choose to advance the calendar 1 or 2 days. Since Yellow only has 1 worker on the gears (on Tikal), advancing 2 days would push no one off, but Yellow decides to advance just 1 day to be cautious. The Tzolk'in gear rotates once. All workers on all gears move forward one space. Green's workers move from space 0 to space 1 on each of their gears. Blue's workers move from spaces 1 and 2 to spaces 2 and 3 on Palenque. Everyone advances.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tzolk'in gear rotates - all workers advance one space

walkthrough.title: Round 2: Yellow Places Workers (New Starting Player)
walkthrough.text: Yellow is now the starting player. He places 2 workers: one on Palenque action space 0 and one on Palenque action space 4 (the lowest unoccupied spaces, since Blue's workers advanced to 2 and 3). That costs 0 + 4 = 4 for action spaces, plus 1 for the two-worker fee, totaling 5 corn.
walkthrough.image_caption: Yellow places workers on Palenque

walkthrough.title: Round 2: Green Picks Up Two Workers
walkthrough.text: Green has all three workers on gears and decides to pick up two, leaving one behind. She picks up the worker from Yaxchilan action space 1, gaining 1 wood. Then she picks up the worker from Tikal action space 1 and uses the action to advance 1 level in Agriculture technology, paying 1 wood (the wood she just earned). She leaves the Palenque worker on the gear to advance further and get more corn later. Excellent planning. The order she picked up workers mattered: she needed the wood from Yaxchilan before she could pay for the Tikal technology.
walkthrough.image_caption: Green picks up two workers, using Yaxchilan wood to pay for technology

walkthrough.title: Round 2: Blue Decides Between Placing and Picking Up
walkthrough.text: Blue has 2 workers on Palenque (now at spaces 2 and 3 after the gear advanced) and 1 available worker. Blue could pick up workers for corn and wood, or place the third worker somewhere new. Blue decides to place the third worker on Tikal action space 0, which costs nothing extra (0 for the space, 0 for placing 1 worker). Smart. Blue is setting up for a technology or building play later.
walkthrough.image_caption: Blue places one worker on Tikal for free

walkthrough.title: Round 2: Red's Second Mistake - Forgetting the Gear Rotation
walkthrough.text: Red has 1 corn left and 3 workers on gears (Yaxchilan spaces 2 and 3, Tikal space 2 after advancement). She decides to pick up the worker from Yaxchilan action space 2, which gives 1 stone and 1 corn. Now she has 2 corn. She wants to pick up from Yaxchilan action space 3 to get 1 gold and 2 corn. But wait, she already has one worker left on Tikal that she is leaving on the gear. Red forgets that the gear will advance again at the end of this round, pushing that Tikal worker from space 2 to space 3. She is planning to pick it up next round at space 3 to construct a building, but she has not calculated whether she will have the resources by then. This kind of planning error is extremely common in Tzolk'in.
walkthrough.image_caption: Red picks up workers but forgets to plan around gear rotation

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Plan for the Gear Rotation
walkthrough.text: The gear rotation is the heart of Tzolk'in. Every worker on every gear advances every round. Before you decide which workers to leave on the gears, mentally add 1 (or 2 if someone is speeding up the calendar) to their current action space. Ask yourself: what action will this worker be on next round? Will I have the resources to use it? Will it be pushed off before I can pick it up? This forward planning is what separates good Tzolk'in players from great ones. Also keep in mind that other players using the Starting Player Space can advance the calendar an extra day, which can catch you off guard.
walkthrough.image_caption: Plan ahead: where will your workers be after the gear turns?

walkthrough.title: Round 2: End of Round
walkthrough.text: No one placed a worker on the Starting Player Space this round, so 1 corn is placed on the current tooth of the Tzolk'in gear. The gear advances 1 day. All remaining workers shift forward. The game continues in this rhythm: place workers, let them advance on the gears, then pick them up at the right moment for the best actions. As the game progresses toward the first Food Day, players need to ensure they have enough corn to feed their workers (2 corn per worker in play).
walkthrough.image_caption: 1 corn added to Tzolk'in gear; gear advances

walkthrough.title: Looking Ahead: The First Food Day
walkthrough.text: The first Food Day is approaching in several rounds. Each player will need to pay 2 corn per worker in play. With 3 workers, that is 6 corn. If you recruited a 4th worker, that is 8 corn. Players who spent all their corn placing workers early, like Red, will need to harvest corn from Palenque or use the market at Uxmal before the Food Day hits. Players who cannot feed all their workers lose 3 VP per unfed worker. Planning your corn income around Food Days is one of the most important skills in Tzolk'in.
walkthrough.image_caption: Food Day approaching: budget 2 corn per worker

walkthrough.title: Red's Third Mistake: Not Preparing for the Food Day
walkthrough.text: As the Food Day approaches, Red has been focused on collecting resources from Yaxchilan and building technologies at Tikal. She has accumulated some stone and gold, but only 2 corn. With 3 workers in play, she needs 6 corn to feed them all. She cannot feed even 2 workers with just 2 corn. Red will lose 3 VP for the one worker she cannot feed, and she may need to beg for corn on her next turn, losing a temple step. If she had planned for the Food Day earlier, she could have picked up a Palenque worker for corn instead of chasing resources.
walkthrough.image_caption: Red has resources but not enough corn for the Food Day

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Food Days Are Non-Negotiable
walkthrough.text: Food Days come every quarter of the game and you cannot avoid them. Always count the rounds until the next Food Day and make sure you will have enough corn to feed your workers. A good habit: about 3-4 rounds before a Food Day, start pivoting toward corn production. Use Palenque for harvesting, Uxmal action 2 to convert resources to corn, or even just fish at Palenque action 1 for a quick 3 corn. The 3 VP penalty per unfed worker and the temple step lost from begging are devastating. Planning around Food Days is the single most important skill for beginners.
walkthrough.image_caption: Always know when the next Food Day is coming

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: The game continues for a full revolution of the Tzolk'in gear. Each round follows the same rhythm: take turns placing or picking up workers, optionally deal with Food Days, then advance the calendar. As you play, you will develop a feel for the timing: when to place workers early at cheap spots versus waiting for higher-value actions, when to grab resources versus corn, and how to time your crystal skull deliveries to Chichen Itza for maximum points.
walkthrough.image_caption: The rhythm of Tzolk'in: place, advance, pick up, repeat

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen the core loop of Tzolk'in: placing workers cheaply, letting the gears advance them to better action spaces, and picking them up at the right moment. You have watched Red learn three hard lessons: do not overspend on placement, always plan for the gear rotation, and never forget about Food Days. The gear mechanism is what makes Tzolk'in unique. Every worker gets better with time, but wait too long and it falls off the gear. Finding the perfect timing is the puzzle at the heart of this game.
walkthrough.image_caption: You understand the core loop of Tzolk'in

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for corn management and timing advice.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every action space, technology, and monument.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun, and may the gods look favorably on your tribe!

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips for corn management and timing advice
Reference the Appendix for every action space, technology, and monument
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime - tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Corn Is King
walkthrough.text: Corn is the lifeblood of Tzolk'in. You need it to place workers, to feed workers on Food Days, to pay for temple offerings at Uxmal, and to step back to lower actions when picking up. New players consistently underestimate how quickly corn disappears. A good habit is to always know how many rounds remain until the next Food Day and to keep a mental budget. Before placing workers, calculate the total cost including the placement fee. Before picking up, think about whether you need to step back and what that will cost. And always have at least 6 corn (for 3 workers) banked before a Food Day hits.

walkthrough.title: Place Cheap, Pick Up Late
walkthrough.text: The gear mechanism rewards patience. A worker placed on action space 0 costs nothing but will advance to action space 3 in just three rounds, giving you a valuable action for free. The longer you leave a worker on a gear, the more valuable its action becomes. The ideal pattern is to place workers on low-numbered (cheap) spaces and pick them up several rounds later when they have advanced to high-value actions. Resist the urge to place workers on high-numbered spaces. Paying 4 or 5 corn for a single action space is almost never worth it when you could place on space 0 and wait.

walkthrough.title: Spread Your Workers Across Gears
walkthrough.text: A common beginner mistake is loading all workers onto one gear. When you do this, you are forced to pick them all up at the same time on the same action, wasting flexibility. Instead, spread workers across two or three gears so you have options. Picking up from multiple gears on one turn lets you chain actions together: grab resources from Yaxchilan, then use them to build at Tikal. Spreading workers also means you are never forced into picking up workers before they have advanced to good action spaces.

walkthrough.title: Get a Fourth Worker Early
walkthrough.text: You start with 3 workers and can acquire up to 6. Getting your fourth worker from the bank using Uxmal action 3 is one of the strongest early-game moves. More workers means more actions per round and more flexibility in your timing. The downside is that each extra worker costs 2 corn to feed on Food Days. But the extra actions easily pay for the feeding cost. Aim to recruit your fourth worker within the first five or six rounds. A fifth worker is good if you have strong corn production, but a sixth is rarely worth the feeding burden.

walkthrough.title: Invest in Technology Early
walkthrough.text: Technologies give permanent bonuses for the rest of the game. The earlier you invest, the more times those bonuses pay off. Agriculture level 1 gives 1 extra corn every time you harvest, which adds up enormously over a full game. Resource Extraction level 1 gives extra wood from Yaxchilan and Palenque. Architecture level 1 gives 1 corn every time you construct a building. These small bonuses compound over 26 rounds. Level 1 costs only 1 resource block, so try to get at least one technology to level 1 in the first few rounds.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Temples Carefully
walkthrough.text: Temple position affects you twice per age during Food Days, plus the bonus for highest player at end-of-age scoring. Being on a low step costs you VP at end-of-age scoring, and being on the bottom step actually loses VP. Climb to at least the middle steps on all three temples by the first end-of-age scoring. The top step of each temple can only hold one player, so getting there early locks everyone else out. Also remember that reaching the top step flips your player board to the lighter side, re-enabling the calendar speed-up privilege.

walkthrough.title: Time Your Actions Around Other Players
walkthrough.text: Tzolk'in has significant player interaction through the shared gears. When another player places a worker on a gear, they take the lowest available space, which can block you. If you want Palenque action space 0, you need to get there before someone else does. Similarly, the Starting Player Space is critical for controlling the calendar speed. Pay attention to which gears other players are investing in and whether they might use the Starting Player Space to advance the calendar by 2 days, which would push your workers forward faster than you planned.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Ignore Crystal Skulls
walkthrough.text: Crystal skulls are one of the most efficient sources of victory points in the game. Placing a skull on Chichen Itza gives you VP, a temple step, and sometimes a free resource. A single well-timed skull placement can be worth 10 or more VP when you factor in the temple bonus. Getting skulls from Yaxchilan action 4 or through Theology technology, then delivering them to Chichen Itza, is a strong strategy at any player count. But there are only 13 skulls in the game, so if you want this strategy, start collecting early before they run out.

walkthrough.title: The Crystal Skull Rush
walkthrough.text: One of the most powerful strategies in Tzolk'in is an aggressive crystal skull approach. The idea is to invest early in Theology technology and Yaxchilan worker placement to accumulate skulls quickly, then deliver them to Chichen Itza for massive VP payouts. Theology level 1 lets you look one space ahead on Chichen Itza. Theology level 2 lets you spend a resource to climb a temple after placing a skull. Level 3 gives you an immediate skull from the bank. With full Theology, a single Chichen Itza placement can give you 8-13 VP plus two temple steps. The key is speed: there are only 13 skulls, and in a 4-player game they go fast.

walkthrough.title: Temple Dominance Strategy
walkthrough.text: Temples are scored twice during the game (end of each age), and the highest player on each temple receives a significant bonus. A temple dominance strategy focuses on climbing all three temples aggressively using Uxmal action 1 (3 corn for 1 step), Tikal action 5 (1 resource for 1 step on two temples), and crystal skull placements. The top step of each temple can only hold one player, so reaching it first locks out all opponents. Being first on two or three temple tops at the end of Age 2 can easily swing 15-20 VP with position scoring plus bonuses. This strategy pairs well with starting tiles that grant temple steps.

walkthrough.title: The Resource Engine
walkthrough.text: Resource Extraction technology is underrated. At level 1, you get 1 extra wood from Yaxchilan action 1 and Palenque wood actions. At level 2, you get extra stone from Yaxchilan actions 2 and 5. At level 3, you get extra gold from Yaxchilan actions 3 and 5. With full Resource Extraction, a single pickup from Yaxchilan action 5 gives you 1 gold, 1 stone, 2 corn, plus 1 extra gold and 1 extra stone, totaling 2 gold, 2 stone, and 2 corn from one action. This fuels building construction, technology advancement, and Chichen Itza skull placement. The resource engine is strongest in longer games where you can repeatedly cycle through Yaxchilan.

walkthrough.title: Architecture and Building Focus
walkthrough.text: Architecture technology makes buildings cheaper and earns you VP for constructing them. At level 1, you get 1 corn per building constructed. At level 2, you can waive 1 resource block from a building's cost at Tikal, or pay 2 fewer corn when building with corn at Uxmal. At level 3, you score 2 VP per building. With Tikal action 4, you can construct two buildings in one turn (applying architecture to one of them). This strategy wants lots of resources and a steady stream of buildings. Pair it with Resource Extraction for the blocks and use the building bonuses (farms, shrines, civic buildings) to snowball your advantages.

walkthrough.title: Agriculture and Corn Economy
walkthrough.text: Agriculture technology turns Palenque into a corn factory. At level 1, you get 1 extra corn per harvest. At level 2, you can harvest corn even when no Corn Harvest Tile is visible. At level 3, you get 1 extra corn from fishing. With full Agriculture, fishing at Palenque action 1 gives 4 corn (3 base + 1 bonus), and harvesting corn gives 3 extra corn on top of the tile value. This strategy lets you place workers aggressively because you always have corn to pay placement costs and food days. It also lets you use Uxmal action 4 to build with corn instead of resources, and at game end your massive corn stockpile converts to VP at 1 per 4.

walkthrough.title: Starting Player Space Control
walkthrough.text: The Starting Player Space is often overlooked but incredibly powerful. Besides collecting accumulated corn (which grows by 1 each round no one takes it), it gives you control of turn order and the option to advance the calendar 2 days instead of 1. Advancing 2 days effectively gives every worker on every gear an extra round of advancement. This is devastating when you have workers on high-numbered action spaces that are about to reach free-choice territory. It also disrupts opponents who planned for a 1-day advance. Use this privilege strategically in the middle of each age when you have workers well-positioned. The cost is flipping your player board to the darker side, which you can reset by reaching a temple top.

walkthrough.title: Monument Selection and Timing
walkthrough.text: Monuments score only at game end, so evaluate them based on what strategy you are already pursuing. The technology monument (score 3 VP per tech level) is excellent if you are investing heavily in multiple tracks. The building monument (score 2 VP per building and 2 VP per monument) rewards a wide building strategy. The harvest tile monuments (4 VP per corn tile or 4 VP per wood tile) reward heavy Palenque use. The temple monument (score end-of-age temple VP again) is powerful if you have strong temple positions. Ideally, claim a monument in the second half of the game when you know whether your strategy supports it. Monuments can only be built with Tikal action 4, so plan your Tikal workers accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Timing the Age 1 to Age 2 Transition
walkthrough.text: The transition between ages is a critical inflection point. All remaining Age 1 buildings are swept from the board and replaced with Age 2 buildings. If you have been investing in resources and technologies in Age 1, you should be ready to construct more powerful Age 2 buildings in the second half. The mid-age temple rewards give you resources and crystal skulls based on your temple positions, so having good temple positions at the midpoint funds your Age 2 plans. Players who neglected temples in Age 1 often find themselves starved for resources when Age 2 begins. Conversely, the end-of-Age-1 building sweep can be used to your advantage by not building Age 1 buildings you do not need, knowing they will be replaced.

walkthrough.title: Worker Count Optimization
walkthrough.text: The number of workers you have in play directly affects your feeding costs and action throughput. Three workers cost 6 corn to feed. Four cost 8. Five cost 10. Six cost 12. Each extra worker gives you one more action per round, but you need to generate enough corn to feed them all. The sweet spot for most strategies is 4 workers, recruited via Uxmal action 3 in the first quarter of the game. Five workers is viable if you have Agriculture technology and Palenque access for corn. Six workers is a trap unless you have multiple farm buildings. One monument scores VP based on workers in play (0, 6, 12, or 18 for 3, 4, 5, or 6 workers), so if you see that monument available, factor it into your recruitment plan.

walkthrough.title: Endgame VP Maximization
walkthrough.text: In the final rounds, shift your focus to pure VP output. Crystal skulls you still hold are worth 3 VP each. All resources convert to corn at the market rate, and all corn converts at 1 VP per 4. This means a gold block is worth about 1 VP from conversion, which is low, so it is almost always better to spend resources on buildings, technologies, or temple climbs rather than hoarding them. The most efficient endgame plays are: constructing a monument that scores well with your strategy, placing a final skull at Chichen Itza for direct VP plus a temple step, climbing temples for the final end-of-age scoring, and picking up a last Palenque worker for corn to feed workers and minimize penalties. Avoid placing workers in the last few rounds unless they will advance to a worthwhile action before the game ends.

entries.definition: The large central 26-tooth gear that represents the Mayan calendar. It rotates 1 day counter-clockwise at the end of each round, driving all five smaller location gears. One full revolution equals the entire game. Four teeth are marked as Food Days with colored stickers.

At the end of every round, the Tzolk'in gear advances 1 day (or 2 if a player uses the Starting Player Space privilege). This pushes all workers on all five location gears forward by 1 action space. Workers on the highest space are pushed off and returned with no action.

On your turn, you may place 1 or more available workers onto gears. Each worker goes on the lowest-numbered unoccupied action space on the chosen gear. Cost = action space number + placement fee (based on number of workers placed, shown on your player board).

On your turn, you may pick up 1 or more of your workers from gears. For each, perform the action at its current space, pay 1 corn per step to use a lower action, or do nothing. Workers are resolved sequentially in any order you choose.

The highest-numbered action spaces on each gear (6-7 on small gears, 10 on Chichen Itza). When picking up a worker from here, you may perform any action on that gear without paying extra corn for stepping back.

A cost from your player board based on how many workers you place on one turn. 1 worker = 0 extra, 2 workers = 1 extra (total cost shown in bold), 3 workers = 3 extra total. This is paid on top of the action space costs.

The basic commodity of the game, serving as both food (to feed workers on Food Days) and currency (to place workers, pay for temple offerings, step back to lower actions, and build with corn at Uxmal). Corn is unlimited. At game end, every 4 corn scores 1 VP.
A resource block represented by wooden pieces. Gained from Palenque (harvesting wood tiles) and Yaxchilan (action 1). Used to pay for technology advances, building construction, and temple steps.
A resource block represented by wooden pieces. Gained from Yaxchilan (actions 2 and 5). Used to pay for technology advances, building construction, and temple steps.
A resource block represented by wooden pieces. Gained from Yaxchilan (actions 3 and 5). Used to pay for technology advances, building construction, and temple steps. Generally the scarcest resource.
A collective term for wood, stone, and gold. When a cost says 'pay 1 block,' you may pay any one of the three resource types. Corn and crystal skulls are not resource blocks.

A special material gained from Yaxchilan action 4, Theology technology, or mid-age temple rewards. Placed on Chichen Itza for VP and temple steps. Remaining skulls at game end score 3 VP each. Exactly 13 exist in the game.

One of five location gears. Focused on food and wood production. Action 1: Fishing (3 corn). Actions 2-5: Harvest tiles for corn or wood. Actions 6-7: Free choice. Burning the forest at actions 3-5 gives corn but angers the gods.
Gain 3 corn. This action never depletes. With Agriculture level 3, you gain 4 corn instead.
Take 1 Corn Harvest Tile from the corresponding jungle group to gain 4 corn. This group has only corn tiles (no wood on top). With Agriculture tech, gain extra corn.

Take the top Harvest Tile from this group. Wood Tile: gain 2 wood. Corn Tile (if wood already taken): gain 5 corn. You may burn the forest (discard wood, take corn, step down a temple). With Agriculture tech, gain extra corn when harvesting corn.
Take the top Harvest Tile from this group. Wood Tile: gain 3 wood. Corn Tile: gain 7 corn. You may burn the forest (discard wood, take corn, step down a temple).
Take the top Harvest Tile from this group. Wood Tile: gain 4 wood. Corn Tile: gain 9 corn. You may burn the forest (discard wood, take corn, step down a temple).

One of five location gears. Focused on resource gathering. Action 1: 1 wood. Action 2: 1 stone + 1 corn. Action 3: 1 gold + 2 corn. Action 4: 1 crystal skull. Action 5: 1 gold + 1 stone + 2 corn. Actions 6-7: Free choice.
Gain 1 wood. With Resource Extraction level 1, gain 1 additional wood (2 total).
Gain 1 stone and 1 corn. With Resource Extraction level 2, gain 1 additional stone.
Gain 1 gold and 2 corn. With Resource Extraction level 3, gain 1 additional gold.
Gain 1 crystal skull from the bank. No effect after all 13 skulls have been taken. With Theology level 3, gain 1 additional crystal skull.
Gain 1 gold, 1 stone, and 2 corn. With Resource Extraction, gain extra wood (L1), stone (L2), and/or gold (L3) as applicable.

One of five location gears. Focused on technology advancement and building construction. Action 1: Advance 1 tech. Action 2: Build 1 building. Action 3: Advance 1-2 tech. Action 4: Build 1-2 buildings or 1 monument. Action 5: Climb 1 step on 2 temples (1 block). Actions 6-7: Free choice.
Advance 1 level on one technology track. Pay the appropriate number of resource blocks (1 block for level 1, 2 for level 2, 3 for level 3, or 1 block for the repeatable bonus at max level).
Construct 1 building from the building row. Pay its resource cost. Architecture technologies may apply. Deal a replacement building from the current age at end of your turn.
Advance 1 or 2 technology levels. The 2 advances can be on one track or split across two different tracks. Pay the appropriate cost for each advancement separately.

Construct 1 or 2 buildings, or 1 monument. If constructing 2 buildings, architecture technologies apply to only one of them. Architecture technologies never apply to monuments. Monuments are only buildable with this action.
Climb 1 step on two different temples. Costs 1 resource block (any type). You cannot use this to climb 2 steps on the same temple.

One of five location gears. The commercial center. Action 1: Temple offering (3 corn). Action 2: Market exchange. Action 3: Recruit worker. Action 4: Build with corn. Action 5: Perform any action from another gear (1 corn, not Chichen Itza). Actions 6-7: Free choice.
Climb 1 step on one temple of your choice. Costs 3 corn.
Exchange corn and resources as many times as you want using the resource exchange table on the board. You can convert corn to resources and resources to corn at the posted rates.
Take 1 worker of your color from the bank and put it in front of you. You now have an additional worker to place on future turns. No effect once you have all 6 workers in play.

Construct 1 building by paying entirely in corn: 2 corn per resource block in the building's cost. You cannot use actual resources, and you cannot build monuments with this action. Architecture technology discounts apply differently here (2 fewer corn instead of 1 fewer resource).
Pay 1 corn to perform any one action from the Palenque, Yaxchilan, Tikal, or Uxmal gear (not Chichen Itza). This costs 1 corn in addition to any costs of the chosen action.

One of five location gears. The sacred skull placement site. Larger gear with action spaces 1-10. Each action has an oval for one crystal skull. Once a skull is placed, that space is locked for the rest of the game. Actions give VP, temple steps, and sometimes resources. Action 10: Free choice.

A special space between Palenque and Chichen Itza. Costs 0 corn. Benefits: take accumulated corn immediately, worker returns at end of round, Starting Player Marker changes hands, may advance calendar 2 days (if board is lighter-side-up and no workers would be pushed off a gear by the extra day).

The brown temple on the left side of the board, dedicated to the rain god Chaac. The shortest of the three temples. Mid-age rewards include stone. End-of-age rewards are VP based on step position. Bottom step gives negative VP.

The yellow temple in the middle of the board, dedicated to the feathered serpent god. Medium height. Mid-age rewards include wood and crystal skulls at higher steps. End-of-age rewards are VP based on step position.

The green temple on the right side of the board, dedicated to the serpent deity. The tallest of the three temples with the most steps. Mid-age rewards include wood at lower steps and stone at higher steps. End-of-age rewards are VP, with the highest points at the top.

The highest step on each temple, shaped like the god's symbol. Only one player can occupy the top step of each temple. Reaching it flips your player board to the lighter side, re-enabling the calendar speed-up privilege. If you are displaced by angering the gods, another player may then claim it.

Certain actions anger the gods, forcing you to move down 1 step on one temple of your choice. Triggers include: burning the forest at Palenque (discarding a Wood Tile for the Corn Tile beneath), and begging for corn. You cannot choose a temple where you are already at the lowest step.

A special round that occurs 4 times per game (marked by stickers on the Tzolk'in gear). After all turns, players must feed workers (2 corn each) and receive temple rewards. Two types: brown-orange (mid-age, resource rewards) and blue-green (end-of-age, VP rewards).
On the first and third Food Days, players receive resources and crystal skulls based on temple positions. You get the reward depicted on your current step AND all steps below on each temple.

On the second and fourth Food Days, players receive VP based on temple positions. You score only the VP on your current step (not lower steps). The highest player on each temple gets a bonus (lower-left number at end of Age 1, upper-right at end of Age 2). Ties split the bonus at half, rounded down.

If you have 2 or fewer corn at the start of your turn, you may discard all corn and take 3 from the bank. This angers the gods (step down 1 temple). Cannot beg if at the bottom of all temples. In the rare case of no workers on gears, no corn, and no ability to beg, the gods allow 1 free worker placement.

One of four tech tracks. Level 1: +1 corn when harvesting corn (Palenque 2-5). Level 2: +2 corn when harvesting corn, and can harvest corn even without a visible Corn Tile. Level 3: +1 corn when fishing (Palenque 1). Max bonus: advance 1 step on any temple. Benefits are cumulative.

One of four tech tracks. Level 1: +1 wood from Yaxchilan 1 or Palenque wood actions. Level 2: +1 stone from Yaxchilan 2 or 5. Level 3: +1 gold from Yaxchilan 3 or 5. Max bonus: take 2 resource blocks of your choice. Only applies to Yaxchilan and Palenque actions, not other sources.

One of four tech tracks. Level 1: +1 corn when constructing a building (Tikal 2 or 4, Uxmal 4). Level 2: waive 1 resource block (Tikal) or pay 2 fewer corn (Uxmal) when building. Level 3: +2 VP when constructing a building. Max bonus: +3 VP. Does not apply to monument construction. When using Tikal action 4 for 2 buildings, architecture applies to only one.

One of four tech tracks. Level 1: on Chichen Itza, may choose to perform the action 1 space ahead (instead of current or lower). Level 2: after a Chichen Itza action, may spend 1 resource block to climb 1 temple step. Level 3: gain 1 extra skull when getting a skull from Yaxchilan 4. Max bonus: take 1 crystal skull from the bank.

To advance on a tech track: level 1 costs 1 resource block, level 2 costs 2 blocks, level 3 costs 3 blocks. Blocks can be any mix of wood, stone, and gold. At max level (3), each additional 'advance' costs 1 block and grants the max bonus.

The first set of buildings available during the first half of the game. These tend to be cheaper and provide foundational bonuses. At the midpoint (after the 2nd Food Day), all remaining Age 1 buildings are removed and replaced with Age 2 buildings.

The second set of buildings available during the second half of the game. Generally more powerful and more expensive than Age 1 buildings. Dealt after all remaining Age 1 buildings are swept at the midpoint.

Buildings that grant an immediate, one-time bonus when constructed. Bonuses can include corn, resources, technology advances, temple steps, victory points, extra workers, market exchanges, or free actions. Three subtypes: Civic (green border), Tombs (gray border), and Shrines (blue border).

A type of building that provides an ongoing benefit during Food Days. Farms reduce the corn cost to feed specific workers. One farm makes 1 worker free to feed. Another makes 3 workers require no corn. A third reduces each worker's feeding cost by 1 corn. Two copies of the third type make all feeding free.
Single-use buildings with a green border. When constructed, you immediately gain all depicted bonuses. Some monuments score VP based on the number of civic buildings you have.
Single-use buildings with a gray border. When constructed, you immediately gain all depicted bonuses. Some monuments score VP based on the number of tomb buildings you have.
Single-use buildings with a blue border. When constructed, you immediately gain all depicted bonuses. Some monuments score VP based on the number of shrine buildings you have.

Special tiles available from the start of the game (6 in 4p, 5 in 3p, 4 in 2p). Score VP at game end based on specific conditions. Can only be constructed with Tikal action 4. Architecture technology does not apply to monument construction. Not replaced when taken.
Score 4 VP per tomb (gray-bordered) building or monument you constructed. This monument itself counts as one.
Score 4 VP per civic (green-bordered) building or monument you constructed. This monument itself counts.
Score 4 VP per shrine (blue-bordered) building or monument you constructed. This monument itself counts.
Score 2 VP per building you constructed and 2 VP per monument you constructed.
Score VP for each monument constructed during the game (by any player). Rate: 6 VP per monument in 2p, 5 VP in 3p, 4 VP in 4p. This monument counts itself.
Score 4 VP per Corn Harvest Tile you collected during the game.
Score 4 VP per Wood Harvest Tile you collected. Burned wood (discarded via forest burning) does not count.
Score VP based on number of workers you have in play (not in the bank): 0 for 3 workers, 6 for 4, 12 for 5, 18 for all 6.
Score 3 VP per level of technology you have across all four tracks. A marker on the starting space counts as 0 levels.
Score VP for technologies in which you reached level 3: 9 VP for one, 20 for two, 33 for three or all four.
Choose one temple. Count the number of steps your marker is above the starting space. Score 3 VP per step.
In each temple, score the VP indicated by your current step (the same scoring as end-of-age blue-green Food Days, but without the bonus for highest player).
Score 3 VP per crystal skull placed on Chichen Itza during the game, regardless of who placed it.

Tiles placed on the jungle fields near Palenque during setup. Corn Harvest Tiles are on the bottom; Wood Harvest Tiles are on top (for actions 3-5). Taking a tile depletes that field. Keep collected tiles in front of you for potential monument scoring.

21 tiles dealt 4 per player at setup (keep 2, discard 2). Provide starting bonuses: corn, resources, crystal skulls, technology advances, temple steps, extra workers, or VP. Some have ongoing effects. In 2-3 player games, remaining tiles determine dummy worker placement.

A two-sided board for each player. The lighter side is the starting side. When you use the Starting Player Space privilege to advance the calendar 2 days, flip to the darker side. Reaching the top step of any temple flips it back to the lighter side, re-enabling the privilege.
A colored marker placed on the scoring track around the board edge. Tracks VP throughout the game. VP can go negative (from unfed workers or bottom-step temple penalties).

Workers in unused player colors placed on gears during 2-3 player setup. They block action spaces and are never pushed off. They stay on the gears for the entire game. You can use player markers instead of workers to distinguish them from active player pieces.
A table on the game board showing exchange rates between corn and resources. Used with Uxmal action 2 (market exchange) and during final scoring when converting resources to corn.

After the 4th Food Day: (1) Convert all resources to corn via market exchange rate. (2) Score 1 VP per 4 corn. (3) Score 3 VP per remaining crystal skull. (4) Score monument effects. Tiebreaker: most workers on gears, then shared victory.

At end-of-age Food Days, each player scores VP based on their current temple step. Bottom steps give negative VP. The highest player on each temple gets a bonus (split if tied). These VP are scored twice during the game (end of Age 1 and end of Age 2).

When placing a crystal skull on Chichen Itza, you receive immediate VP ranging from about 4 to 13 depending on the action space, plus a temple step and sometimes a resource. These VP are scored during the game, not at the end.
On Food Days, each worker you cannot feed costs you 3 VP. A worker costs 2 corn to feed. If you have 5 corn and 3 workers, you feed 2 (4 corn) and lose 3 VP for the third. VP can go negative.
The first half of the game, spanning from the start to the 2nd Food Day (end-of-age blue-green). During Age 1, only Age 1 buildings are available in the building row.
The second half of the game, spanning from the midpoint to the 4th Food Day (end of game). During Age 2, only Age 2 buildings are available. Age 2 buildings are generally more powerful.

The privilege of advancing the Tzolk'in gear 2 days instead of 1, available only to the player on the Starting Player Space. Requires lighter-side-up player board and that the extra day would not push any worker off a gear. Using it flips your board to the darker side.
